title: Is Google Doing Enough to Win the A.I. Race?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Ready to walk and talk?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: What are we talking about?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: We're just scenic, scene setting. Can you be less close?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: No, I'm asking, I was told to shut up because I ruined too much of the show earlier, so I'm like what what am I allowed to talk about?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Can you sack up and just give me some scenic B-roll here?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I fully sacked.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I have to do everything around here, I swear to God. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well Kevin, the keynote just wrapped up here at I/O 2024. What did you think?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yes, this is like the Coachella of capitalism, the warped tour of the web, the Lollapalooza of links... I could keep going.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Please don't.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And they are doing a lot. We heard about everything from like agentic search to these new like video editing models and products they have, all the way to Demis Hassabis at the end of the keynote declaring that we are in the foothills of the singularity.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: When we look back at this time, I think we'll realize that we were standing in the foothills of the singularity.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Quite a kicker.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah and that puts us on pace by I/O 2025 to be at the base camp of the singularity. I love that Google's version of the Steve Jobs like 'one more thing' is like 'one more thing, the singularity!'

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: A great way to end. So yeah, what was your standout, what did they announce that caught your eye?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I mean, they have said that they just made the biggest change to search in 25 years. I think time will tell if that is truly as significant a change as they're suggesting because when we saw it in the demos, it just looked like the box grows if you type more into it. But you know, they're also saying that by this summer, they're going to be generating custom user interfaces based on your query and a bunch of other stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I think like a through line through everything that they discussed here was coming this summer or coming to a group of trusted testers. And with these kind of productivity tools, until they're in your hands, you don't really know if anything has changed in your life or not.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, there was a lot of focus on agentic stuff like agentic coding, they have this new agentic search mode which basically is sort of the fancy upgrade to Google Alerts. You can have it sort of tell you when there's like a new home listed on Zillow or a new baseball score you might care about or something like that. I was really interested that they seem to be betting very big on cost and speed. So their new model that they talked about today was Gemini 1.5 Flash, which is the newest version, but it's also they say four times faster and much cheaper than the other leading frontier models. And so I think Google's strategy of kind of betting on scale and distribution...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Efficiency and like making their models as efficient to serve as possible is really the direction they're going in. Like they don't seem to care about having the absolute most cutting edge models as long as they can serve them cheaply and quickly to billions of people.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I mean, I think they care about it. I think that they just haven't built it yet, you know. Fast and cheap is what you talk about when it isn't the best. And so that's what we're getting this year.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: But you know, I am interested in trying these new agents. I'll say, I- I tried to use the new agent, I said, 'Let me know if Kevin Roose writes a good story,' but it hasn't sent me anything. And so I don't know if it's broken or if the AI isn't working or what.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it must be- I'm writing good stories constantly, so something must be going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I think my real curiosity is like which of these agents are going to find true product market fit. Like which is the agent that is going to get a billion users? Because we saw them show off what is effectively their version of OpenInterpreter, which definitely like had a big moment earlier this year, but it's still not clear to me that that is an actual mainstream use case. Like I have to say, like of everything they showed off, like I'm not like dying to like have, you know, Gemini OpenInterpreter working overnight on my behalf because I don't know what I would ask it to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think that was actually one of my favorite parts because I am currently running a Claude swarm on my open laptop.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Doing what? What's it doing?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And what they have said is that you can now do that through Gemini Spark, this new agent thing, and it will run in like a virtual machine on the cloud so you don't have to like keep your laptop cracked like an insane person.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: But Kevin, please, what are you doing with your Claude swarm?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I can't tell you.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: You can't even tell me? Because you're not doing anything.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I'm ushering in the singularity.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: You're generating recipes.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I'm building a bioweapon, but I don't want to say that in this crowd.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: They get sensitive about that sort of thing around here.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So what was the vibe of I/O this year?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I mean, I honestly, the only people that I have talked to are like reporters and people that work at Google. So like I feel like we have not really had a chance yet to talk to developers and see what is on their minds. Clearly they have a lot of new toys to play with and I'm sure they're excited about that. But you know, as I said, until you get your hands on them, you don't really know if your life has changed.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I feel like they have confidence in their sort of place in the front of the pack. I don't think they believe that their models are the best in the world yet, but if they're cheaper and faster and they can do more stuff... they have this new omni model that can take in video as well as images and text. So I feel like they're sort of doing the spray and pray approach to AI development, which is you just do everything everywhere all at once and hope that something hits. And it's worked for them.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and I guess I will say one thing about the vibe here at I/O this morning, Kevin, which is this is the only recent gathering of a large...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: number of people where mentions of AI do not produce a large chorus of boos. Um, this—this was a crowd where people actually seemed like they wanted to hear about new AI developments and as we saw many many AI generated images and videos for almost two hours, there was nary a boo to be heard.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I agree. Not a lot of mention of the AI backlash or any of the things. I think they're just sort of hoping that these new products are so useful to people that they'll sort of forget about all their objections, um, and then, you know, of course comes Demis Hassabis to talk about the singularity just to remind you that none of this is normal, that we are experiencing something very weird.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: All right, well, I think those are some good early thoughts. We have a lot more to do at I/O, uh starting with probably uh hitting that uh lunch line in the press tent.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I saw some ube croissants on my way in. I'm still trying to figure out what that means, but uh I'm gonna go try it out.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: All right Kevin, well, we are back in the studio the day after I/O and we have a few hours before we head back down to interview Sundar Pichai. But before we do that, you have spent a little bit of time playing around with this new Gemini 1.5 Flash model. So what have you been doing with it?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So I did try to use anti-gravity their kind of coding assistant to put the model through some of my own evaluations and it seems like two things are true. One is it's very fast. Uh it's very fast and you don't hit your sort of token limits uh at all like very quickly, uh which is a nice contrast to some of the other coding models that I've used. Um and the second thing is that it just doesn't seem like that much of an improvement over what is out there now. I think you know there have been some people on my feeds talking about how they are feeling disappointed by the new Gemini model, there's some indications that it's maybe not as good as Gemini 1.5 Pro at some coding evaluations, um but it is much faster and I think the other thing that people are reacting to is the cost. So the cost of Gemini 1.5 Flash while lower than other kind of frontier models is significantly more expensive than the last version of Gemini Flash. So people are upset about that. But I think in general my sense is like it's a good model, it's a pretty good model, it's not like blowing me away, um but it is quite fast and if you are a company that's burning, you know, billions of tokens a day you might be interested in that, but for the average person I would say like nothing about it has made me want to switch to it as my daily driver yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I have to say like this class of models has always just felt like it's not for me, right? Like I am somebody who is always willing to wait a little bit longer and to spend a little bit more to get the best answer, right? Like I would sort of rather do that than just sort of have like instant answers from a very cheap model.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You like to surround yourself with kind of awe-inspiring intelligence.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yes, that's exactly right.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Which is why we're hosting this podcast together.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: That's exactly right. And so, I mean, look, I think for a very wide variety of use cases, a model like this is going to be perfectly fine. If you're, you know, the average Gemini user and you're just trying to pass sixth grade for free, I think this model is going to be a lot of help. Um, but, you know, the sort of more vocal AI power user crowd, which are the people that you're going to find on social media sort of putting these models through their paces on day one, does seem to be a little disappointed here. And I do think it's notable that a model that was sold to us yesterday largely on the strength of its low price does not actually seem to be as cheap as maybe we were told.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And I think part of this is just that the cycle of kind of I/O and developer conferences is maybe out of step with how these models are actually being built. I've been hearing some whispers from folks at Google that they are working on something that is more powerful, um, but that it just may not have been ready in time for I/O this year. So I think that's one tricky thing about doing these conferences once a year is kind of every deadline has to line up at exactly the same time or else you end up looking like you're behind.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I mean, I think the real test for Google, I mean, at least the next real test for Google will come next month when it puts out 3.5 Pro, which I think will sort of be at a similar class of the frontier models that we have today. And that's when I'm going to sort of be putting the model through all of its paces and trying to figure out, okay, like what part of my daily workflows might this kind of thing slot into?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah. All right. Casey, what is your verdict on I/O? Did it meet your expectations?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: It felt like a base hit to me. You know, I think that if they ship everything that they showed off and it works basically as advertised, like, I think that there is some good stuff in there. I don't think that there was a single product that made me think if I can't use this, like today, I will be in extreme physical distress. Um, but yeah, overall I thought, you know, solid set of announcements. What'd you think?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think that basically meets my expectations. I talked to one reporter who was walking around at I/O and who said it was like mid. Sort of like, you know, it's like good. It's good. It's a better position than they were in a couple years ago at I/O when they were basically scrambling to get Bard out the door and, and everything looked like it was going sideways. So they found their footing, but it is still not clear whether they are producing a model that will truly be state of the art.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: All right. So that is some of our thoughts and early hands-on impressions of Gemini 1.5 Flash. Now let's head down to Google to talk to Sundar.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Okay. Are you driving?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I thought we were going to take a Waymo.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Okay.

highlights: During the keynote, Google DeepMind’s Demis Hassabis declared that we are currently standing in the "foothills of the singularity," highlighting the company's perspective on the transformative trajectory of AI.
Google introduced what it calls the biggest change to Search in 25 years, moving toward AI-generated summaries and custom user interfaces that adapt to specific user queries.

A major theme of the event was "agentic" AI, featuring tools designed to act on a user's behalf for complex tasks like autonomous coding, monitoring real estate listings, or managing data in a cloud-based virtual machine.

Google is prioritizing efficiency and scale over raw model power with the release of Gemini 1.5 Flash, which is designed to be faster and cheaper to serve to billions of users than existing frontier models.
While the new models offer impressive speed, early hands-on impressions suggest they may not yet represent a significant leap in reasoning or quality compared to existing state-of-the-art tools.
summary:
Google I/O 2024 centered on the aggressive integration of generative AI across the company’s ecosystem, highlighted by the debut of Gemini 1.5 Flash and a major overhaul of Google Search. Hosts Casey Newton and Kevin Roose discuss the event’s emphasis on "agentic" AI—tools designed to act on behalf of the user, such as automated coding assistants and advanced search alerts. A recurring theme was Google’s strategic shift toward efficiency; the company appears to be prioritizing speed and cost-effectiveness over absolute state-of-the-art performance, aiming to leverage its massive distribution network to bring AI to billions of people quickly.

Despite the high-profile announcements and Demis Hassabis’s provocative claim that the world is in the "foothills of the singularity," the hosts noted a significant gap between the demos and reality. Many featured tools are not yet available to the public, and early testing of Gemini 1.5 Flash suggests that while it is remarkably fast, it may not yet outperform existing frontier models in quality or cost. Ultimately, the hosts characterized the event as a "base hit"—a solid performance that proves Google has regained its footing in the AI race, even if it has yet to deliver a "must-have" product that fundamentally changes the daily lives of power users.
description:
Google wrapped up its annual I/O developer conference this week, unveiling a slew of new A.I. tools, from Gemini 3.5 Flash to Google Spark. Kevin Roose and Casey Newton, the hosts of "Hard Fork," break down the biggest moments of the event, and how they're thinking about Google's next chapter.     

Hard Fork is a weekly look into the future that's already here. Hosts Kevin Roose and Casey Newton explore stories from the bleeding edge of tech.

FOLLOW US ON SOCIAL

Subscribe to Hard Fork!

Amazon - https://music.amazon.com/podcasts/7c7fe198-e6a8-41a8-b0fe-1d46b976dcd8/hard-fork
YouTube Music - https://music.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLB9gMmtMLXxsa8C0PzHFL2tJFh7FrKrYD&si=Fwb9Uwon1ZneNsBp
The New York Times - https://www.nytimes.com/column/hard-fork

“Hard Fork” is hosted by Kevin Roose and Casey Newton. Produced by Rachel Cohn and Whitney Jones. We’re edited by Vjeran Pavic. Engineering by Chris Wood and original music by Dan Powell, Marion Lozano, Sophia Lanman and Rowan Niemisto. Fact-checking by Caitlin Love. Video production by Jake Nicol, Chris Schodt and Sawyer Roque. Podcast Art Direction by Benjamin Wilkerson Tousley. Podcast Art & Animations by Julian Hespenheide. Additional Motion graphics by Phil Robibero. Thumbnails by Julia Moburg and Tara Godvin. Special thanks to Paula Szuchman, Pui-Wing Tam and Dahlia Haddad.
topics: Key announcements and highlights from Google I/O 2024
The introduction and performance of the Gemini 1.5 Flash model
Major AI-driven updates to Google Search
The development of AI agents and agentic search capabilities
Google's AI strategy focusing on speed, scale, and efficiency
